examples.title: Turing Machine for L = {0^n 1^n}
examples.description: Algorithm to accept strings of n zeros followed by n ones
examples.code:
// TM for L = {0^n 1^n | n >= 1}
// States: q0 (start), q1 (matching 0s), q2 (return), q3 (accept), q4 (reject)
// Tape alphabet: {0, 1, X, Y, B}

BEGIN
// q0: Find first unmarked 0
IF δ(q0, 0) THEN
 WRITE X
 MOVE RIGHT
 GO TO q1
ELSE IF δ(q0, Y) THEN
 MOVE RIGHT
 GO TO q2
ELSE IF δ(q0, B) THEN
 GO TO q3 (accept)
ELSE
 GO TO q4 (reject)

// q1: Find matching 1 for marked 0
IF δ(q1, 0) or δ(q1, X) THEN
 MOVE RIGHT
 STAY IN q1
ELSE IF δ(q1, 1) THEN
 WRITE Y
 MOVE LEFT
 GO TO q2
ELSE
 GO TO q4 (reject)

// q2: Return to find next 0
IF δ(q2, Y) or δ(q2, 0) or δ(q2, X) THEN
 MOVE LEFT
 STAY IN q2
ELSE IF δ(q2, X) THEN
 MOVE RIGHT
 GO TO q0
ELSE
 GO TO q4

END
examples.explanation: Uses marking technique: Replace 0 with X and find matching 1 to replace with Y. Continue until all matched. Accept if reach blank after all 1s matched, reject on mismatch.

examples.title: Universal Turing Machine Simulation
examples.description: Shows how UTM simulates any other TM
examples.explanation: UTM interprets the encoding of any TM M and simulates its behavior on input w. This is the theoretical foundation of stored-program computers.
examples.timeComplexity: O(t × k) where t = steps of M, k = simulation overhead

examples.title: Halting Problem Decider (Impossible - for proof)
examples.description: Illustrates the paradox used in undecidability proof
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the diagonalization argument. The paradox shows no algorithm can decide halting problem for all programs.
